summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – AIRPORT WATCHLIST ORDER – Overseas travel – Where final parenting orders were made 6 months ago – Where orders provided father with sole decision-making for major long-term issues for the child – Where no time between child and mother except as requested by the child – Where mother prohibited from removing the child from the Commonwealth of Australia – Where father specifically permitted to remove the child from the Commonwealth – Where mother found out father intended to relocate with the child to their homeland and had child’s name placed on the Airport Watchlist – Orders made removing the child’s and father’s names from the Airport Watchlist

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

(Revised from Transcript)

Amended pursuant to r 10.14(b) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) on 10 March 2026.

BRASCH J:

This is a parenting matter where both parties seek urgent orders about their younger child, X, leaving the Commonwealth of Australia, or not.
The mother seeks to prevent the child’s departure from the Commonwealth and has had the child’s name entered on the Airport Watchlist by filing an Initiating Application seeking same on 19 January 2026.
The father seeks her application be dismissed, and his and the child’s names be removed from the Airport Watchlist.

Background

It is common ground both parents are from Country B.
The children are Ms C born in 2008 (and is now 18 years) and X born in 2013 (collectively “the children”).
There is little by way of background in the material before me. Doing the best I can, the mother Ms Robinson was born in 1974. She lists her occupation as Customer Service. She does not list herself as an Australian citizen, ordinarily resident in Australia or domiciled in Australia. The father deposes that the mother is on a Bridging Visa and has requested Ministerial intervention to stay in Australia.
The mother says the father was born in 1971 and is an Australian citizen. The father says he, his partner and X are citizens of Country B but are becoming Australian citizens too, which will facilitate travel from Country B to Australia to visit the older child Ms C (now 18 years and beyond the Court’s reach).
In Part C of her Initiating Application sealed 19 January 2026, the mother says they were married in 2007, separated on 15 March 2019 and divorced in 2022.
The father adds they were married in Country B and moved to Australia with their two children in 2017. X was accordingly about three or four years old. Both children were born in Country B. He says the mother has a son from a previous relationship, Mr D born in 2002, who, to the best of the father’s knowledge, lives in Country B with his father. Mr D spent time with the father and children in Australia in July 2024.
The father has re-partner with Ms E. They commenced cohabitation in August 2020 and were married in 2023.

Final Orders made September 2025

On 1 September 2025, a judge in Division 2 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia made final parenting orders, the gist of which is:
The father have sole decision-making responsibility for long-term decisions relating to the care, welfare and development of the children;
The children live with the father;
They spend no time with the mother except as may be requested by the children;
The mother be permitted to send letters/cards and gifts to the children once every three months, but the father be at liberty to vet communications and only pass them on to the children if he deems them appropriate;
The mother be restrained from removing the children from the Commonwealth of Australia and their names be placed on an Airport Watchlist for that purpose;
The children be permitted to travel outside the Commonwealth of Australia using an Australian travel document in the company of the father or his authorised representative; and
Order 15 provides, “That to be clear, the Airport Watch List Order does not apply to the children travelling alone, or with the father, [Mr Heyden] born [in] 1971”.
The mother tried to appeal the orders, but the appeal did not advance to hearing. The September 2025 Orders were plainly made within the best interests rubric of Part VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).
Some preliminary observations are warranted with respect to the final best interest orders of September 2025:
(a) Ms C turned 18 years in 2026 and is now beyond this Court’s jurisdiction – she is a young woman and can make her own decisions and chart her own course in life;

(b) The father was conferred with sole decision-making responsibility for long-term decisions relating to the care, welfare and development of the children (now only relevant to X. Section 4 of the Act defines major long-term decisions to include:

(e) changes to the child’s living arrangements that make it significantly more difficult for the child to spend time with a parent.

(c) The effect of the sole decision-making order is that the father is entitled to make such decisions with respect to X;

(d) There is no time for the mother, save as the children may request;

(e) The orders allow the father and children to leave the Commonwealth of Australia; and

(f) The orders do not require the father, or more so the child, to live in a specific country.

What has happened since?

On 19 January 2026, the mother filed an Initiating Application seeking new parenting orders including the following interlocutory and final orders:
Urgent interim order preventing [X] from being removed from Australia until the court can consider the relocation issue.
...

5. An order permitting me to spend time with [X] before any relocation.

...
Respectfully, those matters were determined by a different judge by reason of the final orders made in September 2025.
In her affidavit of the same date, the mother deposed:
On or about 30 December 2025, I became aware through a mutual acquaintance that my ex-husband, the father, had sold their house [in] November 2025 and was planning to relocate to [Country B] with our 12-year old daughter, [X].
8. I have never been notified of any intention to relocate the children.

...

29. The orders allow me to send letters, cards and gifts every three months.
If the father relocates to [Country B] without providing an address, I will be unable to comply with or benefit from the orders.

...

36. The Relocation Is Not in [X’s] Best Interests:

37. The relocation is sudden, undisclosed, and destabilizing.

38. It provides no discernible benefit to the child.

39. It poses extreme educational, emotional, and psychological risks to [X].

40. It severs essential family relationships.

...
The final orders do not require the mother be notified of the father and/or child/ren leaving the Commonwealth of Australia. I do accept though that the order for her to send cards, letters and gifts will be frustrated if the father does not provide an address or PO Box like address for her to do so. The father says he will do this. That is a deficiency that can be remedied by an order.
On 21 January 2026, a judicial registrar of the Court made various orders including this preamble:
UPON THE COURT’S OWN INITIATIVE IN CHAMERS, [sic] AND UPON THE COURT NOTING: When an application is filed seeking a Watch List Order, the Applicant can immediately provide a sealed copy of the application to the Australian Federal Police (AFP). This may need to be accompanied by the Family Law Watchlist Request Form available in the Family Law Kit on the AFP website AND FURTHER NOTING the Court does not send the application to the AFP. Family Law Watchlist and the Australian Federal Police – AFP.

(Emphasis in original)
On 23 January 2026, the mother filed an Amended Application in a Proceeding and an affidavit. In that Amended Application in a Proceeding she sought orders, including:
Pursuant to section 67ZAF of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), the name of the child, [X] (born [in] 2013), be placed and remain on the Family Law Airport Watch List at all points of international arrival and departure in Australia, and that the Australian Federal Police prevent the child from departing Australia with any person until final orders or further order of the Court.
I am not familiar with a s 67ZAF of the Act. However, I completely understand what the mother is seeking. The mother sought other orders in consequence of that to prevent the child leaving Australia.
In her “Supplementary/Amended” affidavit of the same date, she deposed to further information she had received about the father’s relocation, including the sale and leasing of a residence, mail returned to sender, and that the father and child have Country B passports. At paragraph 44, she again stressed the urgency of the travel issue.
On 25 January 2026, the mother filed a second Amended Application in a Proceeding and affidavit. In the Amended Application in a Proceeding she sought “urgent interim procedural and protective orders” about service on the father and:
6-4. In the event of a Family Law Airport Watchlist interception involving the child [X] [sic] (DOB: [...] 2013), that the child be released into the care of the Applicant forthwith, or as soon as practicable, pending further order of the Court.

7-5. That all existing orders made in these proceedings, including the Family Law Airport Watchlist Order dated 21 January 2025, remain in full force and effect.
The mother sought the child be “released into her care” in order 6/4; respectfully, the decision about with whom the child lives (and departures from Australia) was made just over five months ago.
In her affidavit of the same date, she confirmed X’s name was on the Airport Watchlist and deposed to issues of service on the father, and various other issues to which she had previously deposed about the relocation. Curiously, she deposed:
The orders sought in the Application in a Proceeding are sought solely to preserve the status quo, ensure procedural fairness, and protect [X’s] welfare pending further order of the Court.
Respectfully, the status quo is that the child live with the father, he exercise sole decision‑making (including for relocation) and no time with the mother save by the child’s request.
Given the father has been allocated sole decision-making responsibility and is permitted to leave the Commonwealth of Australia with the child, it is hard to see the material change of circumstances in any of her affidavits as required by s 65DAAA of the Act. But that will be for consideration on another day if the father presses that part of his application once this discrete issue is determined. What I am considering at this hearing is the parties’ urgent applications about Airport Watch listings.
On 28 January 2026 the father filed a Response to Final Orders, affidavit by him and a Notice of Risk. In his response he too sought urgent orders, and, relevant to what I am hearing today:
That the father and the child be removed from the Watchlist insofar as they have been added as a result of the Application filed by the Applicant on 19 January 2026, and that the orders in relation to the Watchlist revert to the Final Orders made 1 September 2025.
Relevantly, he deposed:
[X] has not spent any time with her mother since 15 August 2022 and has been resistant to having any relationship with her mother since this time.
...
[Ms C] has made her own decision to remain in Australia and as she is an adult, I support her decision. I am satisfied that appropriate arrangements are in place for [Ms C]. She has asked that I do not share her address or contact details with [Ms Robinson], and I respect her wishes.
Since the final orders were made, neither of the children have requested to spend time with the mother.

...
I acknowledge that I have made arrangements to return to [Country B] with [X] and my wife.
To the best of my understanding, I have not breached the final orders in attempting to relocate to [Country B].
All of my extended family still live in [Country B], including my mother, brother, niece and my cousins. My mother and [Ms E’s] mother are both getting older and have health issues. We would like to return to [Country B] to be closer to our family and to support our mothers.
I have not seen any of my relatives, except my mother, since moving to Australia and I have not returned to [Country B] in 9 years. My mother visited me in Australia on one occasion in early 2023.

30. In late October 2025, [Ms E] told me her mother required [...] surgery.
My mother has [health] problems and she lives alone. This is being monitored by her medical practitioners and her condition is worsening. I would like to spend time with her and provide some assistance.

32. I think it is important for [X] to spend time with her grandmother as well.

...
He deposed to having better job and lifestyle opportunities in Country B, but given he has been granted sole decision-making, including for relocations, the primary judge obviously trusted he would make appropriate decisions. Country B was where the parties lived upon marriage and for a considerable period of their lives. He confirmed they had sold his partner’s house and added:
We had purchased tickets to [Country B] for [Ms E], myself and [X] to depart on [...] January 2026.
[Ms E] and I have been staying in an Airbnb property with [X] since 20 January 2026. We have one suitcase each with some clothes for travel and a few personal items.
All of our remaining belongings are currently in a storage container and have already departed on a ship to [Country B].
At 5:26pm on [...] January 2026, I received an email from [Ms Robinson] advising me of her application and providing me with the court documents filed by her.
Our flights were booked for the following morning, so we went directly to the airport to confirm with the Australian Federal Police whether [X] was on the Watchlist and prevented from travelling. Eventually, we were able to speak to an AFP officer who confirmed that [X] had been placed on the Watchlist.
As a result, we have lost the cost of our flight tickets in the amount of $1,300. Due to the late notice, we were unable to obtain a refund or change our flight arrangements.
We had already sold our two motor vehicles, so we have been renting a car and have extended our stay at the Airbnb. We have spent approximately $2,900 for accommodation and the rental car since 20 January 2026.
[Ms E] has closed her business in Australia and neither of us have any income here in Australia.
I estimate that it will cost us approximately $400 per day that we remain in Australia, as we have nowhere to live and will need to stay in Airbnb or hotel accommodation.
Once our belongings arrive in [Country B], I estimate it will cost us approximately $300 per day to store the shipping container until we arrive in [Country B] and secure permanent accommodation.
We are planning to stay with family in [Country B] until we purchase a property.
[X] is no longer enrolled in a school in Australia, as we had planned for her to attend school in [Country B] this year. I am unsure if we would even be able to enrol [X] in a school in Australia now, as we do not have a permanent address.
We have sold her school uniforms and all of [X’s] other school items are currently on the shipping container being transported to [Country B].
The following day he filed an Application in a Proceeding and affidavit of himself and one by his wife, Ms E. The application and his affidavit were in similar terms to his Response and supporting affidavit. His partner, Ms E deposed to her support of the father, the children, the move back to Country B and their plans there. She was born in Country B too.

The urgent listing for today

Given both parties sought urgent orders, the Court accommodated that request as expeditiously as possible. Thus, on 5 February 2026 the matter was transferred to Division 1 by a Senior Judicial Registrar. I then made the following listing order:
The matter is listed for hearing at 2.30 pm (AEST/Qld time) on Friday, 6 February 2026 via Microsoft Teams in relation to the following interim Orders urgently sought by each party with respect to an Airport Watch List:
(a) Order 6 of the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed by the applicant mother on 25 January 2026 and consequential orders 2-5 and 7;

(b) Order 5 of the interim Orders sought in Response to an Initiating Application filed by the respondent father on 29 January 2026; and

(c) Order 4 of the orders sought in the Application in a proceeding filed by the respondent father on 29 January 2026.

Material

I have listed the mother’s and father’s material in the narrative I have just given. One of the mother’s affidavits has annexures and I will mark that as Exhibit 1 as the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) require.

Discussion and disposition

Section 65Y of the Act says that it is an offence to take a child out of the country without the written consent of the other parent, or in accordance with an order of the Court made under Pt VII of the Act.
The latter condition applies here – court orders permitting the father to travel with the children, or now X, outside of Australia have been made:
That pursuant to Section 11(1)(b)(ii) of the Australian Passports Act 2005 and this Order, the children are permitted to travel outside the Commonwealth of Australia using an Australian travel document in the company of the Father or his authorised representative.
That to be clear, the Airport Watch List Order does not apply to the children travelling alone, or with the father, [Mr Heyden] born [in] 1971.
The Court observes that there is no limitation on where the father could travel with the child/ren in the order, or where the children were to live with him.
The effect of X remaining on the Airport Watchlist is that neither party nor their agents are permitted to remove the child from Australia until such time as the order lapses. The Watchlist operates irrespective of any written consent from the other party – such consent not being forthcoming from the mother in any event.
Thus, the father has a discrete application to remove his name and X’s name from the Airport Watchlist maintained by the Australian Federal Police (“AFP”). The mother wants X’s name to remain listed until a further best interests hearing.
Orders pertaining to a child remaining on the Airport Watchlist or permitting the child to travel outside of Australia are parenting orders within the definition of s 64B(2)(i) of the Act.
Thus, in determining the appropriate orders to be made, the best interests of the child are the paramount consideration (s 60CA of the Act).
The best interests of the child are to be determined by an examination of the considerations as set out in s 60CC of the Act, and whilst the Court must “consider” each of the primary and additional considerations in s 60CC, express discussion is not necessary.[1]
It has already been determined by a different judge that it is in X’s best interests that she live with her father, she be able to leave the Commonwealth (with him or alone) and he make major long-term decisions about, inter alia, relocation that would make it harder for the child to spend time with the other parent.
As Austin J said in Isakson & Isakson[2] at [31], “... s 138 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) now protects its [Division 2] decisions from collateral attack and preserves the validity of its decisions until set aside on appeal.”
This is not an appeal.
The best interest orders, made on a final basis quite recently, allow the father to do precisely what he intends – relocate with X to Country B. That is not only the case by virtue of the sole decision-making order, but made clear by the primary judge in Orders 14 and 15 which have been set out.
I cannot see anything in the mother’s material which even hints at a significant change of circumstance as per s 65DAAA(1)(a) [of the Act] which might otherwise give me pause to allow the father to do what the order permits. I also do not consider it in the child’s best interests for the final parenting orders to be reconsidered per s 65DAAA(1)(b) [of the Act]. However, it will be a matter for the father if he wishes to press that s 65DAAA aspect of his Response and Application in a Proceeding once this discrete issue has been determined.
I can understand the mother might be upset with the father’s decision to relocate back to his home with the child, but that is a matter which the Division 2 judge clearly contemplated he could do given the orders she made.
The final orders do not provide for any specific child-mother time. I do though understand that if the child is in Country B and requests to see the mother then that will be practically difficult. The father however deposes that he will be returning to Australia to visit the older child, Ms C, which presents an opportunity for X to see the mother if she wishes.
X is also of an age where if she wanted to communicate with the mother by electronic device, she would no doubt be able to do so.
Further, there is nothing in the father’s material or his wife’s that gives me any concern about their life in Country B or his relocation back to his homeland.
This is not a matter where the considerations in Line & Line[3] are relevant. This is about the father doing what he is allowed to do under the best interests final order.
Now the mother has discovered that the mere filing of an application seeking a Watchlist order (as per the judicial registrar’s preamble extracted above) will cause that to occur with the AFP, I will also make an order that the father can apply on short notice, including weekends and out of hours, if he has any difficulties in doing what the final order permits – leave Australia with the child.
Accordingly, I will make the following orders.
